speaker: interviewer
text: 30th, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Ruth Kurland of Chicago, Illinois. Tape number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Stalingrad because from there they sent people to Asia. You could go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian. Everything what they do is the Russian, the government, the-- the-- the people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was just sleeping in the football field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, outside, it was-- it wasn't cold. It was warm. There was even woman and children [? undressed ?] outside. It was just like everybody knew already, hundreds of people. And one time we were sleeping outside, and soldiers came and took us. [? Eph ?] was with us. From the 30 of us, 15. They grabbed us, and-- and they took us to a ship. And they didn't tell us where-- where we are going. So we ask where are we going? Nobody will tell us. But my brother talked to, there was a young Russian man, he was very nice, and he told him that we are going to a place where the [? Eph ?] factories from fish. And then we've got to work there. And my brother told them that he knows how to paint because my father was a painter, and he learned how to paint. So he said, OK, you come to me. And this young man, he was a foreman there. And he took my brother to work with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took you on a ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us. We were-- were 15, without my brother, they took us not in a ship. It was like a kayak. You know, you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was a small boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: A small boat. And I don't know, took a couple hours. And we came to a place [? Vietvoda ?] and at night, when they put us to-- to sleep, they put nets because there was so many bugs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they house you? How-- where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside. It was summer. It wasn't winter. And the bugs, it was terrible. They didn't have anything to spray and it was-- I was there a couple days, and they-- they ate me up. I was swollen, everything. My hand, my feet, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the fields. And they decided they have to take me back to-- to this little town. So I came back, and my brother told this-- he became his friend. He said, my-- my sister came back from there. She can't work. She was all sick. He said, OK, so she work in the fish factory. There was a lot of Russian woman working. And it was already so cold that my-- my hand was swollen. It was so swollen. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: The water was cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: The water from the fish was-- have to be cold. We have to-- the-- this fish goes here. This is fish goes there. I don't know, the Russian woman were working there for years, it didn't affect them. At night when you have to-- when you went home, they-- they checked you. If you didn't steal anything, if you didn't steal any fish. They was watching you because, you know, hunger, there wasn't a-- a lot of food to eat. Even the Russians didn't have a lot of food to eat. It was a war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they give us to eat? They give us a piece of bread. Not enough. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a day you got a piece of bread, that's all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Give us the bread and we split it two times, or three times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nothing to drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water. There was water to drink. And we stayed there a couple days, and then we decided, my brother and there was a cousin, the others, that we have to get away from there because this was in a corner from the Caspian-- yeah. When Hitler will come, we'll come-- he'll catch us in the site.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the Caspian Sea you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So we had to go to the-- it was Astrakhan and there the ships went to Asia. And we got our-- [? red ?] Russian friend, they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was before the winter was coming already. I don't know what-- what it was. It was already not warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the [INAUDIBLE] in Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long, couple months. And-- and-- and here we went to [? Loncidle ?]. And we came to this big stations. And there all the ships are going. And they didn't look.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ran away from the fish factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, if they catch us, they kill us. We stay there, we die. So what do you have to do? You run away. If you're young, you run. And there we were standing--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was-- was-- did you run to the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the station somebody took us in a little boat. We had a friend from the Russian. And he took us on a boat. And we said, we want to go just to see what's going on. We came. And there was a lot of people, so many people, and it was already cold. And they didn't give us, for us, they didn't give us food, nothing to eat because we prepared whatever we have with-- from the bread. We took half and we-- we made, it's like a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of the bread, you know, it should stay. And we got a little bit and at night, it was very cold outside. So the only thing in the station, they let us sleep women with children. Other things, you have to stay outside. So I went in there and if a woman had two children I borrowed one child and to sleep with the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: You pretended it was your child--

speaker: interviewee
text: That it was mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that they would let you in to sleep there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with the baby. But then they got smarter. They find out that we were-- we were taking babies to sleep, they chase us out. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody was there trying to, hoping to get a boat to go to Asia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one time we stayed and the soldiers came in there, and they took my brother, and they took young men. Where? They didn't know. And I saw that my brother's going. I said that I want to go with him. So they took me too. And they took some more, not too many girls, but you had a brother or fiance went with them. And we were going. They took us in a-- never tell us where we were going. And we walked, walked, then we came to a desert. In the desert, there's nothing, you know, it's desert. And they gave us shovels and said at night, and it was very windy there. He said, you have to dig holes. You stay in the holes for the wind. The wind will protect you. The holes will protect you. Food, they brought us the other day in the morning, something to eat, but it wasn't much.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in that convoy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of-- there was a lot, maybe 50, 60, and everybody digged a-- a hole to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were Russian soldiers around you watching you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they left. They left. They thought we-- where were we able to go. We don't have anything to go. And there was no water. We had to dig for-- for water. And the-- so when-- when we got a full, I don't know how much water I get, there came out a little bit like this water. It was very bad there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there maybe for two days and all of a sudden, it start raining. And it start raining. Where will you go? There was nothing. And we-- we didn't have what to protect ourselves. So we-- we decided that we had to run away from there. Why did you think you were there in the first place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I still don't know why they took us there. What they want to make there, I can't understand. Somebody said they want to make something big for-- for in case Hitler would come. But there was no-- I-- I still don't understand why they got us there. And we would talk that we had to run away from there. They catch us, we are all dead. They shoot us as a deserter. You know, you know what a deserter? At least maybe we'll run away, and we'll be lucky. So we went away. We walked for a long time, and we came to the same station. But at night, you know, you know what's happened, there was a place that we used to-- when it wasn't the war, they used to sell tickets when you got something. So there was a little roof on top of this. Every night--

words.text: when it wasn't the war, they

speaker: interviewer
text: It was on the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not on the station. Where they used to sell the tickets was a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: The box office.

speaker: interviewee
text: Box office. There was a little roof. So my brother and my cousin and I, every night we went up on this little roof so nobody know we're there. We saw what's going on. The soldiers used to come, and-- and they take the people. And there was screaming, and they'd steal the boots from each other. You know, the-- the-- every time another woman was screaming, they-- they stole my boots! They stole these! It was terrible there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat in those days that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we took with us, before we went, we took bread and we made it, I don't know, [NON-ENGLISH] hard, you dry the bread. And we just eat a little bit, we didn't-- one piece a day should hold us. And ships were going, they did-- always took the woman with the children. The officer's woman. So one time, my brother, my cousin, came and said there's a ship we will be able to go up. What did they stole the ship or something. We went up in the ship, there was so many people that there wasn't a place to sit. You stand and-- and it is-- and every time somebody was dying and they threw them in the-- in the ocean. Lot of people died, they threw them in the ocean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the ship going?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was going to Asia. That's what we want to do. And we came and for days we were going there. And then we came when it was, you know, dry. And then we had to go in trains. They put us trains. So still, they didn't took us, they took the wives.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the boat, for days. I don't remember, it was days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the place called where you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Asia, don't remember the place. But they took us with trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was, again, the government agency?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everything was government. You don't do nothing if they don't, but the only thing, we-- we went up in the train from windows because they wouldn't let us in like everybody. And they brought us to Asia in the city [? Shymkent. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: With this train. And when we came in [? Shymkent, ?] they took us on wagons. From all the 15, we got-- we are 10 people. And they-- they took us to a-- a kolkhoz, like the collective places. And this was all Uzbeks, none of them speaks Russian. They all speak Uzbekish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh you were in Uzbekistan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzbekistan. And [SIGHS] they gave us a little shack for all-- we were 10 people for this--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the other five?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We don't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: They got lost on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Split it up, lost on the way, you never know there. And then you have to eat so there was no kitchen, nothing where to cook, what to do. And they gave us corn with a stone-- with a-- a-- a stone that you put the corn, you turned, then it came out from this flour. And then you have to bake a-- whatever, you bake a-- a cookie or something to eat. But my brother was very handy, he made a little kitchen from stones. He put a little kitchen, and we had to, you know, we had to cook. We had to cook, just to-- to make this little cookie. That's all, this was the only thing we ate. But it was very poor. They didn't-- didn't give us enough food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the living condition like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Sleep on the floor. You had-- this-- it wasn't a living condition, I don't what it was. You-- you-- you look from day to day that you'll survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this whole time were you-- did you get any news of what's happening in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No there, we didn't know nothing. No, nothing. And then I got sick with typhus. And my-- my brother got sick and three other boys. We were 10 people in this place. Nine got typhus. One woman, she was-- she was OK, she took care of us. So they took my brother, and the other three boys to the hospital. Now, they put them in a wagon and they took them away, and I never heard of them, I didn't know what's happened to them. It was already winter, and it was cold. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the average age of the people in your group, in your original group from [? Rowne? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 17, 18, 15, all younger people. And one day, they came back from the hospital with big blankets and a hat, that they were OK. And we stayed in this kolkhoz for a while. My brother was very handy. And an Uzbek took him to work with him, to build houses there. Because they had a lot of stones. And because of my brother, they-- they liked him very much. So he said my sister can work with us too. So I did not much. I just stayed with my brother, and they did the work and I was-- you know, as much as I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did the other people in the group do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The field. In the field it was, you-- you work in the morning. And in the daytime, you rest because it's so hot. But in the day time, you cannot work. And then you work again when the sun goes away. Now, there I couldn't sew because the Asian people, the ladies, they wore the long dresses and that's all. They didn't need dress makers there. So we stayed there for a long time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the winter when it became very cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a little bit warmer than in Asia. It wasn't so cold like in Stalingrad there. There was [? some ?] [? worse. ?] And we stayed there for a long time. And then the army came and they took my brother, and they took my cousin, and they took him to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Maybe around-- maybe '42. And [SIGHS] I was crying so much at all these Uzbeks. You know, they were-- they were very primitive. Looking at me, this woman, they were crying looking at me. I got this only brother, and I-- they took him away to the army. And they took him away. And then one of the Uzbek woman came, she said to me, you know, your brother want to run away and they killed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 20 miles to the [INAUDIBLE] borders, and I said, what? I was never on a horse in my life. I took a horse from this girl house, and I went 20 mile. I don't know how, I never learned to go on a horse. And I came, this was the headquarter. And I came to the commandeer, and I said, my brother was here, is he OK? What's happened? She said, he was OK. The [INAUDIBLE] army took him and he left. I don't know why she told me this-- this story and I came back. How would you say the Uzbeks treated you? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, I don't remember, I used to speak Uzbekish, I forgot everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzbeks? Nothing. We were-- we-- we have nothing to eat. We were always hungry. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't ask us, and we didn't tell them. We were people from the war. They don't even know what Jewish is. They didn't know what the-- they were so primitive people-- they didn't know nothing. Especially in the-- in the kolkhoz. When-- when you were a little bit in the town, like Turkestan they understand better, more intelligent people. But in there, there was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other refugees from the war with you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- we were there, and there were three-- three boys, Ukrainian boys, students. I don't know how they came there, but they were there. Very nice boys. And every time that it comes to the kolkhoz, you have to send people to work in the woods or something. Instead of taking there own people they used to send us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they sent us to a place where we cut big trees, very huge trees. And this was already winter. It was cold. And we had a little shack. There-- there was even no door. We stayed there. [? They ?] [? are ?] [? cold ?] or something for the wind. And they didn't give us one time to eat for two days, nothing. And I felt like, oh, I walk-- walked out, and I faint. Because-- and then when I open my eyes, two girls were pouring water on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were two boys, they didn't eat for themselves, saw me the way I'm always dying. They were running maybe 50 miles to the headquarters and said, if you give-- don't give us food, we'll all die there. So they sent us something to eat. From there, I used to go with another girl, [? Viet ?] [? Litsop, ?] and we find we-- that the Uzbeks used to live in tents. And they-- they had more food to eat than we. So we went to exchange this stuff for-- for corn. We walked in the woods maybe 50 miles. Two girls going in the woods. They could kill us. They could rape us. They could do anything with us. We didn't see anybody. And we come close to this-- where all Uzbeks live, [INAUDIBLE]. And a big dog was running. A dog from the woods that can kill people. And it-- the dog was running straight to us, and I thought that's-- we finished. I don't know from where, from a [? sham, ?] a man came, a Uzbek. He called his dog. This was something a miracle because we didn't expect to leave. We went there. We exchanged these for a little bit, and we came back. You think we went again there because [? Inga ?] was so terrible. We had to go-- again there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What food did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us this-- the corn that you make a little bit of flour and they were using the same thing. But they had a lot of things to do, you know. And they used to bring us back to the kolkhoz. And there were a lot of families. They used to cook and eat, and I used to go and bring them water from the lake. I used to bring them water, and I was too shy to say, give me some food to eat. I used to bring the water and go away and just sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you go for [INAUDIBLE], after work and-- and you think maybe they'll give you something to eat. So some people after they do this, they used to say, give me something. I was young and I was too shy, and I didn't say anything. So I worked, and I didn't have anything. From there one time, they sent us to another place, to the railroad. But the railroad, we [? act-- ?] you-- you cannot make a shovel do it. The earth is so hard there that you got like-- they used to call it [? kitman ?], it's like a hack, you hack there. And I didn't have already shoes because I made myself in-- shoes from rags. And it was colder there, and we worked there. And by the railroad, there were beets. At night we used to go and get a couple beets and cook them and eat. But if they would catch us. They would kill us. You're not supposed to-- you know, it's like you take away something from-- We stayed in a house, all the three girls of us. And one day I said I cannot go--

speaker: interviewer
text: What house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian people, used to live, a little house.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were working on the railroad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they put us there to-- to stay. I don't even know where-- where it was because they send you away, you don't know where you are. And one day, I couldn't go to work. I feel like if I go out, I'll I'll die there. It was very cold, and I didn't eat. And I said to the two girls, I don't want to go out. I-- today I will stay without food, but I cannot go and work. So they brought me food at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two girls. This-- the foreman came in to this couple. So the-- the man said to the foreman, you very nice. She didn't work you gave her food to eat. He said, what? She didn't work? Tomorrow I'm not going to give you. You work. Second day I had to work, and he wouldn't give me anything to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after this, we came back to this-- to this kolkhoz. And then we had a friend in Turkestan and she said to us, if you come here-- because if you [? eat ?] kolkhoz, you'll die from hunger, from-- from everything. Did she write to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody came with a wagon. She sent a letter to us. So we had to go. Nobody will give you a ticket. You cannot get a ticket to go on a train and go. Especially we were, you know, refugees from where, but we decided to go. So I think I had a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she tell you about how things were in Turkestan?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said if you'll come here you'll survive better than-- she was afraid to-- to write too much, you know. There was-- if you catch them they-- We took a train, we were four of us. And we got on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a regular train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was a train. We used to stop in this kolkhoz, and we went on outside without tickets. And then the policeman came in and saw me and said, oh, speculant. Speculant is the people who-- who have things illegal and they sell. And I said, I'm not a speculant. I just came in. And he took me to the headquarter of the police, and-- and I had a little, I don't know, it was nothing and so there was nothing there. And he said, now, you go to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the only thing was-- I feel sorry I don't have my friends, I'm by myself. And in a little while, the three girls, they game in too. They thought they speculants. And we-- and every time, they say, we'll take you to-- to Siberia. But I didn't care. Wherever they take me, you know, you're so used to going wherever they send you. Why did they say they were sending you to-- to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they just want to scare us, I don't know why. All the time they said, we're gonna send you to Siberia. And we came in Turkestan and you go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't send you to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just said that.

speaker: interviewee
text: First they have to take you from the train. And you have to go through a-- to the-- like a big commandeer and they have to check you. And we four came in there. And a lot of people, and these men, was Jewish. The commandeer was Jewish. And he saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Jewish man. You can [INAUDIBLE] the other way. And he saw the two of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. And he had to show for the others that he's not nice to us. And he starts screaming, get out of here! I don't want to see you anymore for my eyes. Just disappear. And he let us go. And this Turkestan he let us go. So if we came up, we were smiling. At least he let us go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what do we do now? We have to-- something to-- so-- there was a Russian woman and I, we went to where they used to make shirts for soldiers. It was like a factory. Where were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Turkestan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Turkestan? We had a train for the kolkhoz. It wasn't too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter any other Jewish people on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Turkestan, was a lot people--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, prior to arriving in Turkestan.

speaker: interviewee
text: No on the train, only we four took the chance. But when we came to Turkestan, there was a lot of, we used to call refugees. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was it like when you got there? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got there, first of all, I didn't have to eat. So the two women, two girls off and went to work in the hospital. They took them in, and they needed hospitals, a lot of people, so they had a lot to eat. The Russian woman and I, we went in this factory to work. And they said we cannot give you anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Off in-- making shirts, sewing. And they said, you cannot. You have to stay at least a month till they'll ride you down to the city, then you can get some bread. Well, we had no choice. We worked with nothing. So I had some money and I used to go in the market and buy, every day, half a pound, the-- the carrots.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of money did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, from a long time ago, I always had a little money that I didn't spend. Hold-- I hold it. And I bought a half a pound carrots. And I was living for a month with the carrots. I looked like, I don't know, I was so skinny, bones. And I looked at myself and the bones I was scared for myself. And after the month, they gave us a lot of bread for a month. So it was good, but you cannot eat it at one time. When you start eating right away you can die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have to go to get the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bread is the-- the [? like ?] little paypost in-- where the factory was. A woman she used to give out the bread. Was always a line. You go even for this, you have to stay in the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia, for everything you have to stay in a line. When they give-- when they used to give like soap, you know what kind of soap-- the soap-- like water. And a man used to come and said, what are they giving? You said, soap. He used to take off his hat and wait. Took the soap in his hat. You stay in the back [LAUGHS] there. What were the living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The living-- we were-- we were in a-- in a house. And when it came winter, it was so cold when you put water, it was ice. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it somebody's house that you were boarding?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was somebody's-- they run away, they moved away, I don't know. It was-- it wasn't a good-- in a good condition. But it was ice there. And we worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothes did you have to wear at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My clothes-- I had one time, I-- I fixed a [INAUDIBLE] in a [? matri ?] skirt for myself and for the two girls, and we went to sleep outside. We came in the morning and somebody took away our skirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: You slept outside, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to sleep-- because we was-- in-- in [? some ?] places, it was too hot. So there used to be like a veranda there. You sleep outside. We came in the morning, and we didn't have our clothes. So I had a-- a nightgown, a torn dress. I went to the-- you have to go to work because if not they send you right the way to Siberia. And this dress had patches. I used to every time put a patch more than the dress but themselves. Patches I had from the-- from the-- where I was working. And when I-- I got sick. So they-- they came, and they took me to the hospital.
